questions.text: What is the primary difference between electrosurgery and electrocautery?

answerOptions.text: A) Electrosurgery uses direct current; electrocautery uses alternating current.
B) Electrosurgery involves passive heat transfer; electrocautery uses electromagnetic waves.
C) Electrosurgery uses alternating current to create intracellular heat; electrocautery uses direct heat transfer.
D) Electrosurgery is only used in dermatology; electrocautery is used in all surgeries.

questions.explanation: Electrosurgery uses alternating current (AC) to create intracellular friction by oscillating charged particles, producing thermal effects like vaporization and coagulation. Electrocautery uses direct current (DC) to heat a metal probe; no current passes through tissue.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Which of the following is TRUE about coagulation waveforms?

answerOptions.text: A) They have high frequency and low voltage.
B) They operate with a 100% duty cycle.
C) They are continuous waveforms with minimal lateral thermal spread.
D) They are interrupted waveforms with high voltage.

questions.explanation: Coagulation waveforms are interrupted (modulated) with short, high-voltage bursts and a low duty cycle, increasing the risk of lateral thermal damage and char.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: What temperature range is associated with desiccation and coagulation?
questions.explanation: Around 60–90°C, water leaves cells and proteins denature/reform, producing desiccation and coagulation without vaporization (>100°C).
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Why does the ESU convert wall current to a higher frequency?

answerOptions.text: A) To reduce energy loss through heat
B) To avoid depolarizing nerves and muscles
C) To ensure higher voltage delivery
D) To prevent burns on the return pad

questions.explanation: Raising frequency above ~100 kHz prevents neuromuscular stimulation (seen at 50–60 Hz), allowing controlled thermal effects without depolarization.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes fulguration?

answerOptions.text: A) Deep coagulation through direct contact
B) Cutting through steam formation
C) Superficial coagulation through an electrical arc
D) Thermal damage from capacitive coupling

questions.explanation: Fulguration uses a no-touch arc to create superficial coagulation/carbonization over broad surfaces.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Why is current density important in electrosurgery?

answerOptions.text: A) It determines the waveform type
B) It controls the color of tissue post-dissection
C) It influences tissue heating based on electrode size
D) It affects REM pad adhesion

questions.explanation: Smaller tips concentrate current (higher density) and increase heating/penetration; larger electrodes diffuse current for a shallower effect.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Which of the following situations increases the risk of stray current?

answerOptions.text: A) Short electrosurgical circuits
B) Using bipolar devices
C) Placing the return electrode near the surgical field
D) Return electrode placed far from the surgical site

questions.explanation: Longer current paths raise the chance of unintended flows through alternative routes (e.g., monitoring leads), increasing burn risk.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: What happens if the dispersive electrode starts to peel off?

answerOptions.text: A) The ESU increases voltage to compensate
B) The ESU halts energy delivery and sounds an alarm
C) Only coagulation mode is disabled
D) The active electrode becomes hotter

questions.explanation: REM (Return Electrode Monitoring) detects poor pad contact and stops energy with an alarm to prevent pad-site burns.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Why should metal clamps not be used to secure instrument cords?

answerOptions.text: A) They scratch insulation
B) They interfere with REM function
C) They can conduct stray current and cause burns
D) They cause capacitive coupling

questions.explanation: Conductive metal can route unintended current and burn tissue; use non-conductive clips instead.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Which tissue type is most resistant to electrical conduction?

answerOptions.text: A) Muscle
B) Adipose (fat)
C) Skin
D) Liver

questions.explanation: Adipose tissue has high resistance and low water content, making it a poor conductor compared with vascular tissues like muscle.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Which instrument type does NOT require a dispersive pad?

answerOptions.text: A) Monopolar pencil
B) Bipolar vessel sealer
C) Monopolar hook
D) Monopolar scissors

questions.explanation: Bipolar instruments include both active and return electrodes at the tips; no dispersive pad is needed.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Which electrosurgical mode is most likely to cause charring and sticking?

answerOptions.text: A) Cut
B) Coag
C) Blend
D) Fulguration

questions.explanation: Coag uses high-voltage, intermittent bursts that rapidly desiccate tissue, promoting char and tip sticking.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: What does REM stand for in the context of electrosurgery?

answerOptions.text: A) Radiated Electromagnetic Monitoring
B) Return Electrode Monitoring
C) Radiofrequency Electrosurgical Module
D) Resistive Energy Meter

questions.explanation: REM continuously checks pad contact and disables current if contact is inadequate to prevent burns.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: How can capacitive coupling cause injury?

answerOptions.text: A) By producing excess steam during vaporization
B) By allowing unintended current to transfer between instruments
C) By increasing insulation thickness
D) By halting the duty cycle

questions.explanation: Charge from an active insulated electrode can induce current in nearby conductors, causing unseen burns away from the visual field.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: What is the best way to manage bleeding when holding a vessel with forceps?

answerOptions.text: A) Use coag mode for a faster response
B) Use fulguration to avoid deep injury
C) Use cut mode for a stronger and more even seal
D) Increase wattage in spray mode

questions.explanation: Cut mode’s lower-voltage continuous waveform produces a deeper, more uniform seal compared with rapid superficial desiccation in coag.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: What aspect of electrosurgical waveform primarily determines whether the effect on tissue is vaporization (cut) or coagulation?

answerOptions.text: A) Type of electrode used
B) Contact between electrode and tissue
C) Duration of activation
D) Proximity of the return electrode
E) Patient body mass

questions.explanation: Firm, direct contact concentrates energy and rapidly exceeds 100°C (vaporization/cutting); lighter or intermittent contact favors 60–90°C (coagulation/desiccation).
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: In what scenario is fulguration most appropriately used during surgery?

answerOptions.text: A) Deep vessel sealing during laparotomy
B) Fine dissection near a nerve bundle
C) Superficial oozing over a wide surface area
D) Vaporizing endometriotic implants
E) Achieving tissue desiccation in fatty tissue

questions.explanation: Fulguration is a no-touch, high-voltage arc technique ideal for broad, superficial oozing; it is not for deep sealing or precise cutting.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Which of the following best explains the clinical significance of capacitive coupling?

answerOptions.text: A) It enhances the current’s ability to cut dense tissue
B) It can cause unintended burns via ungrounded instruments
C) It requires direct contact with active electrode to be dangerous
D) It only occurs with reusable bipolar instruments
E) It is always detected by modern REM systems

questions.explanation: Induced currents in nearby conductors can arc to tissue out of view, producing burns; this is not reliably detected by REM.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Why does using the coag setting for vessel sealing risk a weaker hemostatic seal than using cut?

answerOptions.text: A) Coag creates too high a temperature for coagulum to form
B) Cut mode reaches higher voltages than coag
C) Coag mode carbonizes tissue, which prevents sealing
D) Superficial desiccation from coag forms an insulating layer
E) Coag triggers REM to stop energy delivery too early

questions.explanation: Coag’s rapid superficial drying produces an insulating crust that limits deeper energy transfer, yielding a weaker, heterogeneous seal.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.

questions.text: Which precaution helps minimize stray current injury in monopolar electrosurgery?

answerOptions.text: A) Using maximum power settings
B) Placing the dispersive electrode close to the operative site
C) Wrapping cords around a grounded clamp
D) Using capacitive coupling to direct current
E) Avoiding bipolar instrumentation

questions.explanation: Shortening the circuit by placing the pad near the operative field over well-vascularized muscle lowers resistance and unintended current paths.
questions.submodule: Duke OB/GYN. Playing with fire: Electrosurgery basics. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X2Wi2aQpak4. Accessed June 18, 2025.
